content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary string SSS of length NNN. Your task is to check if there exists a substring of SSS which is the binary representation of a prime number.

Formally, check if there exist integers LLL and RRR such that 1≤L≤R≤N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1≤L≤R≤N, and the substring SLSL+1SL+2…SRS_L S_{L+1} S_{L+2} \ldots S_RSL​SL+1​SL+2​…SR​, when treated as a binary integer, is prime.

Print `"Yes"` if such a substring exists, and `"No"` if it doesn't.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each testcase consists of a single line of input, containing a binary string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one string — `"Yes"` or `"No"`, the answer to the problem.


You may print each character of the answer in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of |S| over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T lines follow, each containing a binary string S (consisting only of characters '0' and '1').
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if there exists a substring of S whose binary value is a prime number, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: only substring "1" → value 1 (not prime) → No.\nTest case 2: substring "11" → value 3 (prime) → Yes.\nTest case 3: substring "1101" → value 13 (prime) → Yes.

solution.approach: A prime larger than 2 is odd, therefore any prime binary representation must end with '1'. The value of a binary substring grows exponentially with its length, but we only need to examine substrings up to 20 bits because 2^20 = 1,048,576 and we can pre‑compute primality for all numbers in [0, 2^20). For each position i where S[i] = '1' we build the integer value of the substring S[i..j] incrementally for j = i … min(i+19, n‑1). If any of these values is prime we answer "Yes"; otherwise after scanning the whole string we answer "No". The primality table is built once with a linear sieve up to 2^20‑1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total length × 20) ≈ O(6·10^6) for the whole input. Pre‑computation of the sieve is O(2^20 log log 2^20).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(2^20) ≈ 1 MiB for the prime table plus O(1) extra per test case.
